briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 16, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Health Threats
sections.content: Today, extreme weather phenomena caused by climate change and their severe impacts have been reported in various parts of the world. The strike by Italian delivery workers amidst a heatwave [2] and the increased risk of cognitive decline in old age [5] demonstrate direct threats to human health and working environments. Canadian wildfires [48] and flash floods in Missouri [52] warn of the potential for even more deadly weather anomalies, and it has also been pointed out that the UK is unprepared to cope with heatwaves [60]. In particular, record-breaking heatwaves across Europe and the United States are having a massive impact on daily life, leading to school closures, traffic disruptions, and an increase in hospital patients [83, 85], and research indicating that climate change causes nearly a week's worth of sleep loss globally each year, disrupting people's nightly rest, suggests that the climate crisis is infiltrating even the fundamental aspects of our lives [47, 71, 77].

sections.title: ⚠️ Gap Between Carbon Neutrality Goals and Reality
sections.content: Realistic challenges to achieving carbon neutrality goals have been raised in several areas. Projections indicate that AI and semiconductor megaprojects conflict with carbon neutrality goals, potentially delaying the achievement of National Determined Contributions (NDCs) by up to 14 years [17]. Furthermore, criticisms have continued regarding the discrepancy between carbon neutrality declarations and reality [27], as well as the gap between goal setting and implementation [29]. Specifically, an analysis shows that despite 84% of S&P 500 companies setting carbon neutrality targets, their actual carbon reductions fall short of even half of their goals, indicating that corporate efforts are not meeting the reality [31]. Unexpected side effects in the policy implementation process have also emerged, such as green spaces created for climate change adaptation ironically exacerbating gentrification [3, 12].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Efforts for Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Amidst these crises, various domestic and international efforts to respond to climate change and move towards carbon neutrality are continuing. Domestically, Suseong-gu in Daegu and the city of Karlsruhe in Germany are seeking inter-city cooperation by holding a climate change response forum [4, 9], and Muju County is implementing specific adaptation measures, such as expanding the distribution of sunshade nets to reduce damage to fruit trees [8]. The Korea SMEs and Startups Agency (KOSME) is leading the transition in the corporate sector by expanding carbon-neutral facility investments within supply chains and strengthening cooperation with prime contractors to address Scope 3 emissions [13, 16, 24, 26], and citizen-led carbon neutrality networks are actively operating in Siheung City [18, 20, 23] and Goyang City [22]. Internationally, a photography project mapping climate change solutions is underway in West Africa [67], and Toronto is strengthening its adaptation efforts, exploring ways to design a cooler city in the face of climate change [81].

sections.title: ⚡ Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: News of policies and infrastructure development to accelerate the transition to renewable energy has continued. The Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) is strengthening international cooperation by putting its renewable energy quality certification infrastructure establishment project in Uzbekistan on track [32, 33, 38, 39, 43]. Domestically, power generators and companies can now directly trade renewable energy through an online platform [34, 45], and a PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) marketplace is scheduled to officially launch next month, which is expected to positively impact solar and wind power generation [37, 44]. KDN is leading grid stabilization by resolving the challenges of renewable energy grid connection with AI-powered Energy Storage System (ESS) technology [42], and is expected to play a crucial role in expanding renewable energy. In the National Assembly, Representative Song Ok-joo has introduced the 'Rural and Fishing Village Renewable Energy Income Village Act,' aiming to establish a foundation for income generation through renewable energy utilization in rural and fishing communities [35, 36].

sections.title: 🌍 International Spotlight on Climate Change Impacts and Policy Challenges
sections.content: The international community continues to highlight the widespread impacts of climate change and the policy challenges associated with them. Research has been released indicating that household water bills in some cities could double due to climate change [49], and the phenomenon of eroding global agricultural potential is being mapped through a new platform [70]. Iceland's former Prime Minister warned that climate change is becoming a global health emergency [51], and a new environmental performance index has shed light on sustainability achievements and future challenges [54]. The International Federation of Horseracing Authorities (IFHA) held a Climate Change and Sustainability Summit, continuing discussions on climate response across various sectors, and a major report on the link between disasters and climate change is expected to be released on Thursday, anticipating further discussions [53, 56]. Concurrently, a critical perspective has also been raised in the UK, suggesting that the 'boring parts' of climate change policy are being forgotten [80].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing once again reminds us of the severity of the global climate crisis and the urgent need for multifaceted efforts by the international community to overcome it. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: On the morning of the 14th, when a heatwave advisory was issued, a citizen was observed viewing a fountain at Ogeori Square in Jeonju, Jeollabuk-do. Yonhap News. On Thursday, the 16th, as sweltering heat with sensible temperatures fluctuating around 35 degrees Celsius grips the nation, monsoon rains are expected to fall again, primarily in the southern Chungcheong region. According to the Korea Meteorological Administration, under the influence of a stationary front, monsoon rains will begin in Honam and Jeju Island in the morning, followed by the Chungcheong region in the afternoon, and at night...

summary.phenomenon: On Thursday, the 16th, sweltering heat with sensible temperatures fluctuating around 35 degrees Celsius is prevalent nationwide, with monsoon rains pouring primarily in the southern Chungcheong region, expecting up to 80mm in Honam and Gyeongnam.
summary.cause: According to the Korea Meteorological Administration, the monsoon rains are occurring under the influence of a stationary front.

articles.original_title: Wildfires in Ontario make Toronto air quality worst in world
articles.description: Environment Canada has issued health warnings after sky over city turns yellowSmoke from more than 100 active wildfires in northern Ontario have made Toronto’s air quality the current worst in the world and caused yellow, smoky air in cities across the north-east US.Environment Canada issued health warnings on Wednesday after the sky over country’s largest city turned a sickly yellow and was ranked the worst in the world according to IQAir, the Swiss technology company that racks global air qual

summary.phenomenon: On Wednesday, over 100 active wildfires in northern Ontario resulted in Toronto's air quality being recorded as the worst in the world. The sky over the city turned a sickly yellow, and smoky air spread across cities throughout the northeastern US. Environment Canada issued health warnings.
summary.cause: Smoke from over 100 active wildfires in northern Ontario is the cause.
summary.outlook: Environment Canada has issued health warnings, indicating that the deteriorating air quality could impact public health.

articles.title: Sadiq Khan backs calls for maximum workplace temperature in UK
articles.description: Mayor of London backs calls from unions to update health and safety rules in light of more frequent heatwavesThe mayor of London, Sadiq Khan, is backing calls for a maximum workplace temperature as pressure grows on the government to protect workers from the impact of repeated heatwaves across the UK.The extreme heat has left people struggling to cope as temperatures in some workplaces climb above 40C, causing thousands of schools to close and hospital and transport systems to break down. Contin

summary.phenomenon: Repeated heatwaves across the UK have led to extreme heat, with temperatures in some workplaces soaring above 40C. This has resulted in thousands of school closures and disruptions to hospital and transport systems. London Mayor Sadiq Khan supports the introduction of maximum workplace temperature regulations.
summary.cause: More frequent heatwaves are the cause.
summary.outlook: The government is under pressure to protect workers from the impact of repeated heatwaves, suggesting the need for policy changes such as maximum workplace temperatures.

articles.original_title: Surviving extreme heat increasingly boils down to this: access to air conditioning | Mark Wolfe
articles.description: The next great climate divide will be between countries that have the resources to adapt and those that don’tThis summer, much of the media’s attention has focused on record temperatures across Europe and the United States. Television coverage has been filled with familiar images: heat maps shaded deep red, schools closing, rail lines slowing, wildfires spreading and emergency rooms treating growing numbers of people with heat-related illnesses.Public officials have responded with equally famili

summary.phenomenon: This summer, record temperatures were observed across Europe and the United States, leading to school closures, railway service delays, the spread of wildfires, and an increase in patients treated for heat-related illnesses in emergency rooms.
summary.outlook: In the future, a significant climate divide will emerge between countries with resources to adapt and those without, suggesting that survival in extreme heat will depend on access to air conditioning.
